coredata.dc:title: Preparation of the double cross-linking carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film for food active packaging application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Mingxuan
Xia, Bihua
Qu, Runyi
Dong, Jialei
Li, Ting
Wang, Shibo
Wang, Yang
Dong, Weifu
coredata.dc:description: This paper prepared a new kind of carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film with antibacterial properties. Carbon dots and citric acid were used as cross-linking agents, and polyvinyl alcohol and carboxymethyl cellulose were used as matrices respectively. The mechanical properties, UV shielding performance, thermal stability, antioxidant capability, and antibacterial activities of the carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film were researched. The prepared carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film was applied in the strawberry freshness preservation test. And test results indicated that the carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film could prevent rotting and extend the shelf life of strawberries. This carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film could be applied in the food active packaging field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cross-linking
Composite film
Antibacterial
Packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film
UV shielding performance and antioxidant capability of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film
Antibacterial activity of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film
Freshness preservation ability of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of CDs, b) UV–vis spectrum of CDs, c) the optimum excitation and maximum emission spectra of CDs, and d) fluorescence spectra of CDs.
a) FTIR spectra curves of CDs, physical blending PVA/CMC film, cross-linking PVA-CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite film.
Gel fraction of the physical blending PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite films with different CD contents.
TGA curves of CDs-PVA-CMC composite films with different CD contents.

a) Stress-strain curves of physical blending PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite films with different CD contents, b) Young's modulus of physical blending PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite films with different CD contents.
a) UV shielding performance and b) free radical scavenging rate of DPPH of CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CD content.

Anti-Escherichia Coli results of a) physical blend PVA/CMC film, and b-e) of CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CDs contents; anti-Staphylococcus Aureus results of a’) physical blend PVA/CMC film, and b’-e’) of CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CD contents.
Photograph of food quality test using strawberry with PE film, PVA-CMC film, and CDs-PVA-CMC composite film during storage at 25 °C.
Photograph of food quality test using strawberry with PE film, PVA-CMC film, and CDs-PVA-CMC composite film during storage at 4 °C.
Tensile strength, strain and Young's modulus values of physical blending PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite films with different CD contents.

head.ce:title: Preparation of the double cross-linking carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film for food active packaging application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, school of chemical and material engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper prepared a new kind of carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film with antibacterial properties. Carbon dots and citric acid were used as cross-linking agents, and polyvinyl alcohol and carboxymethyl cellulose were used as matrices respectively. The mechanical properties, UV shielding performance, thermal stability, antioxidant capability, and antibacterial activities of the carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film were researched. The prepared carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film was applied in the strawberry freshness preservation test. And test results indicated that the carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film could prevent rotting and extend the shelf life of strawberries. This carbon dots-polyvinyl alcohol-carboxymethyl cellulose composite film could be applied in the food active packaging field.

Scheme of CDs-PVA-cmc composite film applied for food active packaging.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Foodborne diseases have been the most widely food safety issue worldwide, which is caused by different pathogens, such as bacteria, viruses, and fungi [1]. According to the statistics results of the World Health Organization in 2015, 600 million people around the world are infected by foodborne diseases each year, and 420,000 people die of foodborne diseases [2]. Food packaging materials play an important role in the food safety and quality field. Traditional food packaging materials only serve as a physical barrier to microbial contamination, and they cannot inhibit the reproduction of microorganisms. Therefore, the development of antibacterial food packaging material that could kill bacteria and extend food shelf life is particularly important.

Traditional antibacterial agents mainly contain antibiotics, however, the overuse and misuse of antibiotics may cause bacteria resistant to antibiotics and result in effectiveness weakening rapidly [3]. In recent years, many kinds of nanomaterials have been widely used in the antibacterial field, for example, silver nanoparticles [4], zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles [5], and titanium dioxide nanoparticles [6], all of these nanomaterials have excellent antibacterial properties. The main reason for the excellent antibacterial properties of nanoparticles is that the nanoparticle can target multiple biomolecules at the same time, which can effectively reduce or eliminate the evolution of drug-resistant bacteria and avoid the development of drug-resistant strains [7]. However, metal nanoparticles have toxicity, which could release toxic ions (such as Ag+, Zn2+, and Ti2+) and harm human health [8].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial with 1 nm - 10 nm size [9], and they exhibit excellent biocompatibility, fluorescence properties [10], and antibacterial properties [11], which have been used in biomedicine [12], energy applications [13], and drug delivery [14], catalysis [15], cell imaging [16] widely. There are three kinds of antibacterial mechanisms, one mechanism is that abundant charge groups exist on the surface of CDs [17,18], which could induce bacteria excessive oxidative stress reactions by electrostatically adsorbing and destroying the membrane structure and die eventually; second mechanism is that the surface of CDs could generate a large amount of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), which could destroy bacterial proteins, biomolecules and outer membranes, leading to death eventually [19]; third mechanism is that CDs could be absorbed by bacteria easily due to their small size, and this absorbing could disturb intracellular components and induce bacterial death ultimately [3]. Some researchers have reported the CDs antibacterial experiment, for example, Chai synthesized P-doped CDs by hydrothermal method, which used meta-aminophenol as the carbon source and phosphoric acid as the phosphorus source, and the prepared P-doped CDs exhibited high antibacterial activity [20].

CDs are usually obtained in a solution environment [21], and the prepared CDs are not easy to separate and fix in a solid matrix due to their small size. This feature prevents them from being used as antibacterial solid material directly, and they need to composite with solid matrix material usually. The most common connection between CDs and matrix materials are physical methods, such as blending or adsorption [22], however, these connections are not firmly, and CDs seep out easily.

Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is the carboxymethylated derivative of cellulose, which has excellent film-forming ability and high reactive, which could react with functional groups of CDs (such as amino group) through the active carboxyl group and form the firm chemical bond structure [23]. To improve the stability of loading CDs, the molecule chain network density of CMC needs to increase further. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a kind of edible, non-toxic, biocompatibility polymer material, due to its high cross-linking and excellent film-forming ability, it has been used as a matrix material in the packaging material field widely [24].

This paper fabricated a new kind of double cross-linking CDs-PVA-CMC composite film, PVA and CMC are used as the matrix material, and CDs and citric acid are used as the main cross-linking agents. The mechanical properties, UV shielding properties, thermal stability, antioxidant properties, antimicrobial activity, and food freshness preservation capability of the double cross-linking CDs-PVA-CMC composite film were researched.

PVA (1799, alcoholysis degree 98 %–99 %) was purchased from Shanghai Bide Pharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. CMC (viscosity: 5000–15000 mpa.s, USP grade) was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. D-Anhydrous glucose was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethylenediamine and citric acid (analytical grade) are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Escherichia coli (E.coli, ATCC 25922), Staphylococcus aureus (S.aureus, ATCC 25923) were provided by American Culture Collection Company. Strawberries were purchased from Wuxi local supermarket.

Firstly, 1.90 g D-anhydrous glucose and 1.80 g ethylenediamine were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water, then poured into a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reaction kettle, raised temperature to 180 °C and lasted for 4 h. Secondly, the reaction product was centrifuged with 3500 rpm for 15 min, took the supernatant and dialyzed for 24 h by using 500 DA dialysis bag. The dialyzed liquid was filtered with a 0.22 μm water filter, freeze-dried, and obtained the CDs powder finally.

Dissolve 1.8 g PVA powder in 20 mL deionized water, raise to 95 °C and stir for 1 h, take 0.2 g CMC powder and dissolve it in 20 mL of deionized water, heated at 85 °C for 40 min, added 0.1 g sodium citrate and stirred at room temperature for 1 h, then added 20 mL different concentrations of CDs solutions (0.25 wt%, 0.50 wt% and 0.75 wt%) and 0.02 g EDC-NHS to CMC solution, stirred for 6 h at room temperature. The mixture solution was poured into the glass petri dish with 85 mm diameter, dried at 50 °C for 24 h, and obtained the double cross-linking CDs-PVA-CMC composite film finally. The prepared composite films were marked as 0.25 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film, 0.50 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film, and 0.75 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film. The PVA-CMC physical blend film and the cross-linking PVA-CMC film only with citric acid as cross-linking were used as blank samples. Thermal analysis of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film was measured by the thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA), test temperature ranged from 30 °C to 700 °C, and heating rate was 10 °C/min, nitrogen (N2) protective, and gas flow was 25 mL/min.

The morphology of the prepared CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (STEM, Thermo Fisher, Talos F200X G2, Czech Republic) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The particle size of CDs was analyzed by a nanoparticle size analyzer (Brookhaven, 90Plus PALS, USA). The fluorescence spectrums of CDs were measured by a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, FLS-1000, UK). The UV–vis spectrums of CDs-PVA-CMC composite films were recorded by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Lambda 35, Waltham, MA, USA). The zeta potential of CDs was measured by Nano ZS90 zeta sizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd.) at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs, physical blending PVA/CMC film, CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CDs contents (0 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt% and 0.75 wt%) were recorded by an FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Nicolet, iS10, Waltham, MA, USA). The scanning range ranges from 400 cm−1 − 3750 cm−1, the resolution is 4 cm−1, and it has 32 scan rates. Tensile strength and elongation at break of physical blending PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite films were tested by Universal Testing Machine (5967×, ITW Group Co., Ltd., USA), and initial grip separation was 5 cm and stretch speed was 20 mm/min. The sample was cut into a rectangular strip 1 cm wide and 10 cm long, each sample was tested 5 times and took the average value.

The gel content of the sample was estimated by measuring its insoluble fraction after soaking the sample in deionized water for 24 h at room temperature, and the gel fraction is calculated as formula (1).
(1)
Gel
fraction
%
=
W d
/
W i
× 100 %
where Wi is the initial weight of the dry sample and Wd is the dry weight of the insoluble portion after soaking.

The 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) scavenging rate was tested according to reference 19 [19]. Added 0.1 g CDs-PVA-CMC composite film into 4 mL 0.1 mM DPPH ethanol solution, after reacting for 30 min in the dark environment, record the absorbance at 517 nm of the DPPH solution (A0) and the DPPH solution with the CDs-PVE-CMC composite film (Ai). The DPPH scavenging rate of the CDs-PVE-CMC composite film is calculated as formula (2).
(2)
DPPH scavenging rate
%
=
1 −
A i
/
A 0
× 100 %

Take 50 μL of Escherichia coli or Staphylococcus aureus stock solution from the cryogenic storage room (−70 °C), add 15 mL of sterilized Luria-Bertani (LB) culture medium, and place it in a shaker with 37 °C and 200 r/shake for 6 h to obtain the bacterial suspension. Take out the bacterial suspension and dilute it to 5.0 × 105–10.0 × 105 CFU/mL with sterile ultrapure water. Place the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film (3 cm × 3 cm) into 10 mL dilute bacterial suspension, seal it, and shake at 37 °C and 200 r/min for 12 h. Pour the molten LB solid medium into a sterile polypropylene plate and solidify under UV light for 30 min. Take 100 μL bacterial suspension from the sample shake flask and apply it on the solid medium plate, seal and place in a 37 °C constant temperature incubator for 24 h, and count the number of bacterial colonies. The formula for calculating the antimicrobial activities (R) is calculated according to formula (3).
(3)
R =
B ▬ C
/ B × 100 %
B is the average number of bacteria recovered from the blank sample after 24 h, and C is the average number of bacteria recovered from the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film plate after 24 h.

The food freshness preservation capability of PVA-CMC film with 0.50 wt% CDs was researched. The composite film was analyzed by evaluating the effectiveness of the film to protect strawberries from spoilage. Fresh strawberry was covered in the following materials: (i) polyethylene film labeled as a commercial; (ii) cross-linking PVA-CMC film; (iii) double cross-linking CDs-PVA-CMC composite film. These samples were put on a sterilized dish. Inspecting the strawberry at 0 days, 4 days, 7 days, and 10 days and evaluating the freshness based on odor, texture, and color appearance.

CDs were prepared through the hydrothermal method using d-glucose and ethylenediamine as raw materials, and the prepared CDs are shown in Fig. 1 . The CDs appeared small and round particle shape, and the average diameter of CDs was relatively uniform, the corresponding size was about 8 ± 1.78 nm, and the zeta potential of CDs was +31.62 mV. Fig. 1b shows the UV absorption spectrum curve of the CD solution. It can be seen that the UV absorption peak of CDs at 301 nm, which was related to the n − π* transition of CO and the π − π* electronic transition of conjugated CC, proving that CDs' structural aromatic sp2 domain (C=C) [25]. Fig. 1c shows the fluorescence excitation spectrum curve and fluorescence emission spectrum curve of CDs solution, the optimum fluorescence excitation wavelength at 410 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength at 500 nm. Fig. 1d shows the multiband fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs solution, when the excitation wavelength ranged from 310 nm to 510 nm, the maximum emission moved in the direction of the long wavelength (from 480 nm to 570 nm) gradually. This long wavelength shifting of fluorescence spectra indicated that the particle size distribution of CDs was concentrated [26]. These typical optical characteristics mentioned above provided further evidence for the successful preparation of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of CDs, physical blending PVA/CMC film, and CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CD contents are shown in Fig. 2 . The black curve was the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, it can be seen that the broad peak at 3440 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of OH, and the peaks at 1626 cm−1 and 775 cm−1 were attributed to the deformation vibration of NH, and this results demonstrated the amino groups existed on the surface of CDs; the peak at 1352 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the carbon‑oxygen double bond, which proved that the carboxyl groups existed on the surface of CDs. The red curve was the FT-IR spectrum of the physical blending PVA/CMC film, and the blue curve was the FT-IR spectrum of the 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film. By comparing these two curves, it clearly showed that the peak intensity of the 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film was more obvious than the physical blending PVA/CMC film, this phenomenon was mainly due to the new formation of hydrogen bonds between citric acid and PVA/CMC [27]. The pink curve, green curve, and dark blue curve were the FT-IR spectra of the 0.25 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film, 0.5 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film, and 0.75 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film, these curves were similar with the 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film only appeared a new formation peak at 1710 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of amido bond, this phenomenon indicated that the carboxyl groups of CMC molecules were cross-linked with amino groups of CDs successfully [28]. The Scanning Electron Microscope image of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film is shown in Fig. S1 of Supporting Information.

To investigate the cross-linking effect of CDs and citric acid further, the gel fraction of the physical blending PVA/CMC film and the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CD contents were investigated. Test results are shown in Fig. 3 , the gel fraction of the physical blending PVA/CMC composite film was 60.44 ± 4.79 %, and the gel fraction of the 0 wt% CDs-CMC-PVA film was 70.8 ± 5.21 %, which was higher than the physical blending PVA/CMC film observably, and this result indicated that the PVA was cross-linking with CMC by citric acid successfully. After adding CDs, the gel fraction was improved further. When CD content was 0.25 wt%, the gel fraction of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film was 81.3 ± 3.11 %; when CD content was increased to 0.5 wt%, the gel fraction was increased to 85.65 ± 6.69 %; when CD content was increased to 0.75 wt%, the gel fraction was improved to 88.73 ± 8.79 %. With CD content increasing, the corresponding gel fraction was increased. These results demonstrated that CDs could cross-link with PVA and CMC, and the dual cross-linking agents had a high cross-linking degree. In addition, the swelling rate and water contact angle of CDs-PVA-CMC composite film were shown in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3 of Supporting Information.

Thermal stability is another important property of CDs-PVA-CMC composite film. The thermal stability test results of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite films are shown in Fig. 4 . The CDs-PVA-CMC composite film exhibited three thermal weight loss stages. The first weight loss stage appeared at 30 °C − 150 °C, which was mainly due to the loss of water and low molecular weight compounds; the second weight loss stage appeared at 150 °C − 350 °C, which was mainly caused by the decomposition of PVA and CMC molecular chains. The third weight loss stage mainly occurs at 350 °C − 470 °C, which was mainly caused by the degradation and carbonization of the small molecular chain structure. In addition, the weight loss value reduced with CD contents increasing, which was mainly due to the CDs' decomposition temperature ≥ 500 °C. These results indicated that the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film had high thermal stability.

The stress-strain curves and Young's modulus of the physical blending PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite films with different CD contents were tested by Universal Testing Machine (5967×, ITW Group Co., Ltd., USA), and the test results are shown in Fig. 5 and Table 1 . The tensile strength of the physical blending PVA/CMC film was 28.3 MPa, the strain value was only about 2 mm/mm and the corresponding young's modulus was 639.1 KPa; after adding citric acid, the tensile strength of the 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film was increased to 31.8 MPa, the strain value also increased to 2.2 mm/mm and the corresponding Young's modulus was 651.8 KPa, this increasing was mainly resulted by the citric acid cross-linking effect between PVA and CMC molecules. After adding CDs, both the tensile strength and strain value were improved observably. When CD content was 0.25 wt%, the corresponding tensile strength and strain value were 35.6 MPa and 7.2 mm/mm, and the corresponding Young's modulus was 456.2 KPa; when CD content was 0.5 wt%, the corresponding tensile strength and strain value were 45.4 MPa and 10.2 mm/mm, and the corresponding Young's modulus was 490.1 KPa. The improvement of tensile strength and strain were mainly caused by the cross-linking effect of CDs, their surfaces were distributed large number of hydroxyl groups, amino groups, and carboxyl groups; the reduction of the Young's modulus was mainly caused by the inorganic property of CDs [25]. However, when CD content was increased to 0.75 wt%, the tensile strength and strain value turned to reduce, and the Young's modulus turned to increase. This phenomenon may be result by the over-cross-linking degree and excess of inorganic CDs [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV shielding performance and antioxidant capability of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film
ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet irradiation was detrimental to food quality and nutrition [30], therefore, the ultraviolet radiation-blocking function was essential for food packaging material. In this work, the UV shielding performance of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film was tested by the UV spectrum analyzer, and the corresponding test results are shown in Fig. 6a. When the CD content was 0 wt%, the UV transmittance was 88.76 %, and the corresponding UV blocking rate was 11.24 %; when the CD content was increased to 0.25 wt%, the UV transmittances was decreased to 7.56 %, and the corresponding UV blocking rate was 92.44 %; when the CD content was increased to 0.50 wt%, the UV transmittances of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film was only 0.6 %, and the corresponding UV blocking rate was 99.4 %; when the CD content was increased to 0.75 wt%, and the UV transmittances was 0 %, the corresponding UV blocking rate was reached up to 100 %. These results indicated that the UV blocking rate was increased with the CD content increasing. The main reason for this phenomenon was that the CDs had a high UV shielding effect [31].

Antioxidant capability was an important property of food packaging material. In this work, the antioxidants of physical blending PVA/CMC film and the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CD contents were tested by the free radicals scavenging rate of DPPH, and the test results are shown in Fig. 6b. The free radicals scavenging rate of DPPH of the physical blending PVA/CMC film was 37.15 ± 2.02 %, which may be attributed to the hydroxy group of PVA and CMC [32]. After adding citric acid, the free radicals scavenging rate of DPPH of 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film was 41.93 ± 2.51 %, and this increase in free radicals scavenging rate was mainly caused by the citric acid [33]. When the CD content was 0.25 wt%, the free radicals scavenging rate of DPPH was 42.29 ± 3.19 %; when CD content was increased to 0.50 wt% and 0.75 wt%, the corresponding free radicals scavenging rate of DPPH were increased to 46.23 ± 4.69 % and 47.67 ± 2.61 %, respectively. These results showed that with the CD content increasing, the antioxidant capability was increased correspondingly. The main reason for this phenomenon was that some electron-donating groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) were synthesized on the surface of CDs, and these groups could scavenge free radicals effectively [34]. The free radicals scavenging rate of ABTS was also measured and the test result was shown in Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activities of the physical blending PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite films with different CD contents (0 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 0.75 wt%) were tested against both Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia Coli) and Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus Aureus). Fig. 7a-7e are the anti-Escherichia Coli results of physical blend PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CD contents, and Fig. 7a‘-7e’ are the anti-Staphylococcus Aureus results of a) physical blend PVA/CMC film and CDs-PVA-CMC composite film with different CD contents. It clearly showed that the physical blending PVA/CMC film didn't exhibit any antibacterial activity against Escherichia Coli and Staphylococcus Aureus. By contrast, CDs-PVA-CMC composite film exhibited higher antimicrobial activity. When the CD content was 0 wt%, the sterilization rate of Escherichia Coli was 90.08 %, and the sterilization rate of Staphylococcus Aureus was 98.06 %. This result indicated that the citric acid exhibited antibacterial activity against Escherichia Coli and Staphylococcus Aureus. The mechanism was that the citric acid has carboxyl groups, which were negatively charged and could absorb bacteria and kill them eventually [35]. After adding CDs, the sterilization rate was increased with the CD content increasing. When the CD content was 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 0.75 wt%, the corresponding sterilization rates of Escherichia Coli were 99.97 %, 99.98 %, and 99.99 %, and the sterilization rates of Staphylococcus Aureus with different CD contents (0 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.5 wt%, and 0.75 wt%) were 95.77 %, 95.99 %, 99.97 % and 99.99 %, respectively. The main reason for this high antimicrobial activity was that abundant charge groups exist on the surface of CDs, the CDs could generate a large amount of intracellular ROS and the CDs could be absorbed by bacteria easily [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Freshness preservation ability of the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, strawberry was selected as the research object, the 0.50 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film was used as the packaging material, the polyethylene (PE) film and 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film were used as the control samples. These films were taken to pack strawberries at room temperature (25 °C) and 4 °C for several days and then observe the freshness degree change, the test results are shown in Fig. 8 and Fig. 9 . As shown in Fig. 8, the strawberries were fresh at the primary stage, after storage for 4 days, the strawberry packaged by PE film appeared slightly spoiled phenomenon, and the strawberry coated by 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film appeared overripening phenomenon; while, the strawberry packaged by 0.50 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film was still fresh. After storage for 10 days, the strawberry packaged by PE film spoiled seriously, the strawberry packaged by 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film also appeared a little spoiled performance, and the strawberry packaged by 0.50 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film didn't spoil consistently. As shown in Fig. 9, the strawberries were fresh at the primary stage, after storage for 4 days, strawberries appeared little dehydration phenomenon which were packaged by PE film and 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film, and the strawberry packaged by 0.50 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film didn't change evidently. After storage for 9 days, the strawberry packaged by PE film appeared little spoil phenomenon, the strawberry packaged by 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film appeared overripening phenomenon and the strawberry packaged by 0.50 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film appeared little dehydration phenomenon. After storage for 14 days, the strawberry packaged by PE film appeared more seriously spoil phenomenon, the strawberry packaged by 0 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC film appeared little spoil phenomenon and the strawberry packaged by 0.50 wt% CDs-PVA-CMC composite film didn't spoil consistently. These results indicated that the CDs-PVA-CMC composite film exhibited a high freshness preservation ability, and it could delay food spoilage effectively.

This paper fabricated a new kind of double cross-linking CDs-PVA-CMC composite through cross-linking reaction and casting film-forming method. Due to the dual cross-linking structure and high active CDs, the prepared CDs-PVA-CMC composite film exhibited 88.73 % cross-linking degree, 45.4 MPa tensile strength, high thermal stability (>300 °C), 100 % UV shielding rate, 47.67 % DPPH scavenging rate and 99.99 % antimicrobial activity. This CDs-PVA-CMC composite film could be applied in strawberry packaging, and it delayed the shelf date of strawberries effectively.

Mingxuan Hou: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bihua Xia: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Runyi Qu: Software, Formal analysis. Jialei Dong: Methodology. Ting Li: Resources. Shibo Wang: Supervision. Yang Wang: Supervision. Weifu Dong: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Wuxi Science and Technology Development Fund Project (k20231016), Suzhou Science and Technology Plan Project (SGC202302), Open project of Jiangsu New Optical Fiber Technology and Communication Network Engineering Research Center (SDGC2315) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52273089).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.132939.
